wormwood.title: Grand wormwood
wormwood.intro: Grand wormwood is the defining bitter herb of classical absinthe: silvery, aromatic, and dry rather than sweet.
wormwood.imageAlt: Grand wormwood plant with silvery foliage.
wormwood.history: Wormwood has been used since classical antiquity around the Mediterranean as a bitter herb in wines, tonics, and digestive preparations.
medicinal: Historically used as a bitter digestive and appetite stimulant.
Commonly listed in older herbals as a vermifuge or worm-expelling herb.
Also appears in traditional remedies for sluggish digestion and dyspeptic complaints.
wormwood.role: Wormwood supplies the stern bitter frame that keeps the house style adult and recognizably absinthe.

roman-wormwood.title: Roman wormwood
roman-wormwood.intro: Roman wormwood is softer and finer than grand wormwood and often used for detail rather than brute force.
roman-wormwood.history: Roman wormwood entered the bitter-herb tradition as a more delicate Artemisia for aromatized wines and absinthe-adjacent formulas.
medicinal: Traditionally treated as a tonic bitter in the broader Artemisia family.
Historically associated with digestive formulas and appetite support.
Used in similar bitter-herbal contexts to grand wormwood, but more gently.
roman-wormwood.role: Roman wormwood lets the line keep an absinthe echo without dragging every bottle toward full severity.

anise.title: Green anise
anise.intro: Anise is the seed note that makes absinthe immediately recognizable: sweet, cool, and licorice-adjacent.
anise.history: Anise moved easily between kitchen and apothecary, flavoring cakes, cordials, and digestive preparations before it became central to absinthe traditions.
medicinal: Historically used as a carminative for gas and digestive discomfort.
Traditional herbals also mention it for coughs and breath freshening.
Older household medicine often treated anise seed tea as a warming stomach remedy.
anise.role: Anise is the louche anchor and the quickest route to making a bottle read as absinthe.

fennel.title: Florence fennel
fennel.intro: Fennel shares absinthe's sweet-spice register with anise, but it is rounder and greener.
fennel.history: A long-standing Mediterranean herb, fennel entered food culture, cordials, confectionery, and herbal medicine through seed, frond, and bulb forms.
medicinal: Historically used for indigestion, bloating, and flatulence.
Traditional medicine also associated fennel with coughs and upper-respiratory catarrh.
Many older sources describe fennel as supportive for breath, appetite, and lactation.
fennel.role: Fennel softens the architecture and keeps the line from becoming too sharp or skeletal.

coriander.title: Coriander
coriander.intro: Coriander seed adds a bright, dry, citrus-spice note that can sharpen an otherwise soft absinthe profile.
coriander.history: Coriander is one of the older recorded culinary herbs, appearing in ancient Mediterranean and Asian contexts and later in European spice cabinets and cordial formulas.
medicinal: Historically used as a digestive and carminative seed.
Traditional sources also describe it for stomach comfort.
In some older systems it was treated as a warming spice useful after heavy meals.
coriander.role: Coriander is the clarity lever in the line: it sharpens without becoming overtly citrus peel.

lemon-balm.title: Lemon balm
lemon-balm.intro: Lemon balm softens bitterness with bright green-citrus aroma and a long tradition as a comforting herb.
lemon-balm.history: Known to Greek, Arab, and later European herbal traditions, lemon balm became an apothecary mainstay for cordials, calming waters, and fragrant household uses.
medicinal: Historically used for digestive upset and gas.
Traditional systems also used it as a calming herb for nerves and sleep.
Older herbals frequently mention lemon balm as uplifting and restorative.
lemon-balm.role: Melissa is the house herb for freshness; it appears wherever the line wants green light rather than darkness.

hyssop.title: Hyssop
hyssop.intro: Hyssop gives classic absinthe some of its cool, resinous, green top notes.
hyssop.history: Hyssop became a monastery-garden herb, a culinary seasoning, and a familiar component in liqueurs and domestic remedies.
medicinal: Historically used in teas and syrups for coughs and chest congestion.
Traditional herbals also describe external use on bruises and skin complaints.
It appears as a warming, stimulating expectorant-type herb in older literature.
hyssop.role: Hyssop brings medicinal altitude and old-world green complexity.

angelica.title: Angelica
angelica.intro: Angelica root is a classic liqueur and bitters material: earthy, musky, and structurally useful in tiny amounts.
angelica.history: Angelica became prominent in northern European monastery gardens and early modern apothecaries.
medicinal: Historically used as a digestive, stomachic, and warming bitter.
Traditional uses also include colds, chest complaints, and general weakness.
Older European herbals often treated angelica as both protective and restorative.
angelica.role: Angelica is the structural root across the line, tying volatile top notes back to the floor.

hibiscus.title: Hibiscus
hibiscus.intro: Hibiscus contributes color first, tart floral structure second, and fruit illusion third.
hibiscus.history: Hibiscus beverages and preserved calyces circulate through African, Caribbean, South Asian, and Latin American food traditions.
medicinal: Historically used in cooling drinks in hot climates.
Traditional medicine in several regions used hibiscus for digestive and fever-related complaints.
Roselle preparations also appear in folk practice as a mild restorative tonic.
hibiscus.role: Hibiscus is the chromatic engine of Crimson and the reason it reads jewel-like instead of merely red.

rose.title: Rose
rose.intro: Rose petals bridge the herbal core to more floral and red-fruit directions.
rose.imageAlt: Pink rose blossom on the plant.
rose.history: Roses moved through Persian, Arab, Mediterranean, and European traditions as perfume, confection, medicine, and ceremony.
medicinal: Historically used as a soothing, lightly astringent herb in syrups and conserves.
Rose preparations were traditionally associated with cooling and digestive uses.
Rosehip-rich preparations later became common in household tonic traditions.
rose.role: Rose softens Crimson and turns tartness into perfume.

butterfly-pea.title: Butterfly pea flower
butterfly-pea.intro: Butterfly pea is the chromatic engine behind Detour's blue-to-violet ritual.
butterfly-pea.history: Butterfly pea has a long culinary and medicinal history across South and Southeast Asia, where the flowers color drinks, rice, and sweets.
medicinal: Historically used in Ayurvedic and regional herbal traditions as a memory and nerve herb.
Traditional uses also include calming, cooling, and restorative preparations.
Folk practice has also used the plant in hair, skin, and eye-related applications.
butterfly-pea.role: Butterfly pea turns Detour from a flavor idea into a visual event.

lemon-verbena.title: Lemon verbena
lemon-verbena.intro: Lemon verbena is more incisive than lemon balm, with a sharper leaf-citrus note.
lemon-verbena.history: Native to South America, lemon verbena was brought to Europe by Spanish and Portuguese networks and became useful in teas, perfumery, and liqueur work.
medicinal: Historically used in teas for digestion and post-meal comfort.
Traditional practice also treated it as calming and gently antispasmodic.
In Latin American folk use it appears as a household herb for stomach complaints.
lemon-verbena.role: Lemon verbena sharpens Detour and keeps its citrus story from becoming blurry.

gentian.title: Gentian
gentian.intro: Gentian is one of the archetypal European bitter roots and a direct route to firmer aperitif structure.
gentian.history: Gentian root has long been central to European bitters, aperitifs, and apothecary tonics.
medicinal: Historically used as a digestive bitter.
Often listed for appetite stimulation in older herbals.
A classic component of stomachic liqueurs and tonics.
gentian.role: Gentian hardens Detour into a dry amber bitter profile and helps pull perception away from licorice.

mint.title: Mint
mint.intro: Mint adds cooling perception and perceived sharpness in tiny amounts.
mint.history: Mint has deep culinary and medicinal history across the Mediterranean and Europe in teas, cordials, and household remedies.
medicinal: Traditionally used for digestion and cooling herbal teas.
Often associated with stomach comfort and breath freshness.
Mentha species appear widely in domestic medicinal practice.
mint.role: Mint is a trace-level perception tool rather than a headline herb in this line.

genepi.title: Genepi
genepi.latin: Artemisia genipi and related alpine Artemisia
genepi.intro: Genepi brings alpine restraint and cool aromatic lift to the bleue profile.
genepi.history: Genepi liqueur and alpine Artemisia traditions are closely tied to mountain herbalism and regional digestifs.
medicinal: Historically associated with mountain digestifs and tonic herb traditions.
Used in folk practice for stomachic and warming preparations.
Part of alpine medicinal and liqueur heritage.
genepi.role: Genepi lets Bleue feel alpine rather than merely clear.

elderflower.title: Elderflower
elderflower.intro: Elderflower gives a subtle blossom halo that can keep a quiet profile from feeling bare.
elderflower.history: Elderflower has a long European history in cordials, fritters, and domestic medicinal infusions.
medicinal: Historically used in teas and infusions for colds and fevers.
Associated with gentle diaphoretic and comforting household remedies.
Also used widely in cordial and syrup traditions.
elderflower.role: Elderflower is a trace halo herb for Bleue rather than a foreground flavor.

cornflower.title: Cornflower
cornflower.intro: Cornflower contributes visual and aromatic softness in blue-leaning ritual systems.
cornflower.history: Cornflower has traveled through ornamental, culinary garnish, and gentle household-herb traditions.
medicinal: Historically used in mild eye and skin folk remedies.
More often a decorative and symbolic herb than a major medicinal one.
Sometimes used in teas and floral mixtures.
cornflower.role: Cornflower supports blue visual logic without overpowering the formula.

orange-peel.title: Orange peel
orange-peel.intro: Orange peel links spice and floral systems to anise without collapsing into juice.
orange-peel.history: Orange peel has long flavored liqueurs, marmalades, bitters, and digestive cordials.
medicinal: Historically used in digestif and cordial traditions.
Often treated as warming and stomach-friendly in older preparations.
Widely used in peel bitters and aromatic preparations.
orange-peel.role: Orange peel bridges Crimson's hibiscus-acid line and gives Detour amber citrus warmth.

rosehip.title: Rosehip
rosehip.intro: Rosehip supports hibiscus with tart fruit, tannin, and a softer red-fruit body.
rosehip.history: Rosehips have a long role in preserves, teas, and household tonic traditions.
medicinal: Historically used in syrups and restorative household preparations.
Often associated with tonic and seasonal use.
A common preserve and tea ingredient with medicinal overlap.
rosehip.role: Rosehip makes Crimson feel fruit-complete rather than merely floral-acidic.

cardamom.title: Cardamom
cardamom.intro: Cardamom lifts blends with cool spice and aromatic polish.
cardamom.history: Cardamom has a long history in perfumery, cookery, and herbal preparations across South Asia and the Middle East.
medicinal: Historically used for digestion and fragrant household remedies.
Associated with warming and carminative spice traditions.
Used in both culinary and medicinal aromatic formulas.
cardamom.role: Cardamom gives Crimson sparkle without dragging it into winter-spice heaviness.

grains-of-paradise.title: Grains of paradise
grains-of-paradise.intro: Grains of paradise add warm peppery brightness and salivation without clove-like heaviness.
grains-of-paradise.history: Aframomum melegueta moved through spice trade, culinary use, and cordial traditions as a peppery aromatic seed.
medicinal: Traditionally used in warming spice systems.
Associated with digestive and stimulating spice traditions in older literature.
Often used where pepper-like energy without black-pepper harshness was desired.
grains-of-paradise.role: This is a trace social-warmth spice for Crimson, not a major flavor block.

star-anise.title: Star anise
star-anise.intro: Star anise reinforces sweet-spice body and can support louche in tiny amounts.
star-anise.history: Star anise traveled widely through spice, medicine, and liqueur traditions as a strong sweet aromatic.
medicinal: Historically used in digestive and warming spice traditions.
Often used in teas and aromatic household remedies.
Shares some culinary-medical overlap with anise but is more forceful.
star-anise.role: Star anise is a reinforcing herb in Crimson, not a lead note.

lemon-peel.title: Lemon peel
lemon-peel.intro: Lemon peel pushes a profile toward clean citrus brightness and ritual freshness.
lemon-peel.history: Lemon peel has long been used in cordials, peels, medicines, and perfumery for bright aromatic lift.
medicinal: Historically used in digestif and household aromatic preparations.
Associated with brightening and stomachic uses in older traditions.
Common in peels, bitters, and citrus cordials.
lemon-peel.role: Lemon peel gives Detour its sharp citrus top note and helps pull the profile away from licorice.

grapefruit-peel.title: Grapefruit peel
grapefruit-peel.intro: Grapefruit peel adds bitter citrus complexity and a more adult citrus register than sweet orange.
grapefruit-peel.history: Grapefruit entered modern aromatic and culinary use later than orange or lemon but became important in bitters and aperitif profiles.
medicinal: Historically less central than older citrus peels in European herbals.
Used more in modern tonic and bitter contexts.
Valued for bitter freshness more than deep folk-medicinal identity.
grapefruit-peel.role: Grapefruit peel makes Detour feel adult, bitter-citrus, and less confectionary.

juniper.title: Juniper
juniper.intro: Juniper introduces conifer lift, resin, and the suggestion of a side road toward gin-like terrain.
juniper.history: Juniper has a long history in preserved meats, medicinal spirits, and later gin and aquavit traditions.
medicinal: Historically used in diuretic and digestive traditions.
Appears in warming household and spirit remedies.
Closely tied to medicinal spirit history in Europe.
juniper.role: Juniper is one of Detour's clearest signals that the route has changed.

mastic.title: Mastic
mastic.intro: Mastic adds pine-citrus resin and unusual textural aroma in very small amounts.
mastic.history: Mastic has long moved through Mediterranean confectionery, incense, medicine, and liqueur traditions.
medicinal: Historically used in oral, digestive, and resin-based household remedies.
Associated with stomachic and fragrant resin preparations.
Also used in incense and chewing traditions.
mastic.role: Mastic gives Detour its deliberate oddness and resinous grip.

rosemary.title: Rosemary
rosemary.intro: Rosemary supplies a clean camphoraceous lift that can make a formula feel brighter and more precise.
rosemary.history: Rosemary has long been culinary, symbolic, perfumery, and medicinal across the Mediterranean world.
medicinal: Historically used for circulation, memory, and warming preparations in older herbals.
Common in aromatic household remedies.
Also used as a preservative and strewing herb.
rosemary.role: In Detour rosemary is a trace clarity tool, not a lead flavor.

yarrow.title: Yarrow
yarrow.intro: Yarrow brings a wild, dry, almost field-bitter quality to a formula.
yarrow.history: Yarrow has deep European herbal history in bitter, wound, and tea traditions.
medicinal: Historically used in bitter, wound, and fever folk remedies.
A classic domestic herb in many European traditions.
Used in teas, compresses, and tonic preparations.
yarrow.role: Yarrow helps keep Detour dry and adult when the color ritual could otherwise turn whimsical.

calendula.title: Calendula
calendula.intro: Calendula petals can lend a warm gold-orange color cue without pushing Detour into blue or red ritual territory.
calendula.history: Calendula has long appeared in European household herb gardens, culinary color work, and topical herbal traditions.
medicinal: Historically used in skin and household herbal preparations.
Also used as a culinary petal and colorant in traditional kitchen contexts.
In this line it is treated as color support, not a therapeutic ingredient.
calendula.role: Calendula supports Detour with a warm amber visual field while staying flavor-light.
